title: Addressed To Miss ----, On Reading The Prayer For Indifference, An Ode, By Mrs. Greville.
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And dwells there in a female heart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By bounteous Heaven designן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The choicest raptures to impart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To feel the most refinedן¿½


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dwells there a wish in such a breast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Its nature to forego,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To smother in ignoble rest
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At once both bliss and woe!


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Far be the thought, and far the strain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which breathes the low desire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How sweet soeן¿½er the verse complain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though Phן¿½bus string the lyre.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Come, then, fair maid (in nature wise),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who, knowing them, can tell
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From generous sympathy what joys
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The glowing bosom swell:


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In justice to the various powers
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of pleasing, which you share,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Join me, amid your silent hours,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To form the better prayer.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With lenient balm may Oberon hence
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To fairy-land be driven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With every herb that blunts the sense
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mankind received from heaven.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh! if my sovereign Author please,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Far be it from my fate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To live unblest in torpid ease,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And slumber on in state;


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Each tender tie of life defied,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whence social pleasures spring,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unmoved with all the world beside,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A solitary thingן¿½ן¿½


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some Alpine mountain, wrapt in snow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thus braves the whirling blast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Eternal winter doomן¿½d to know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No genial spring to taste.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In vain warm suns their influence shed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The zephyrs sport in vain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He rears unchanged his barren head,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whilst beauty decks the plain.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What though in scaly armour dressן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Indifference may repel
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The shafts of woeן¿½in such a breast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No joy can ever dwell.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis woven in the worldן¿½s great plan,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fixן¿½d by Heavenן¿½s decree,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That all the true delights of man
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Should spring from sympathy.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis nature bids, and whilst the laws
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of nature we retain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our self-approving bosom draws
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A pleasure from its pain.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thus grief itself has comforts dear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The sordid never know;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ecstacy attends the tear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When virtue bids it flow.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For, when it streams from that pure source,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No bribes the heart can win
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To check, or alter from its course,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The luxury within.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Peace to the phlegm of sullen elves,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who, if from labour eased,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Extend no care beyond themselves,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unpleasing and unpleased.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let no low thought suggest the prayer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! grant, kind Heaven, to me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Long as I draw ethereal air,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sweet Sensibility!


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereן¿½er the heavenly nymph is seen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With lustre-beaming eye,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A train, attendant on their queen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Her rosy chorus) fly;


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The jocund loves in Hymenן¿½s band,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With torches ever bright,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And generous friendship, hand in hand
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With pityן¿½s watן¿½ry sight.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The gentler virtues too are joinן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In youth immortal warm;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The soft relations, which, combined,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Give life her every charm.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The arts come smiling in the close,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And lend celestial fire;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The marble breathes, the canvas glows,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The muses sweep the lyre.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Still may my melting bosom cleave
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To sufferings not my own,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And still the sigh responsive heave
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereן¿½er is heard a groan.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So pity shall take virtueן¿½s part,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her natural ally,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fashioning my softenן¿½d heart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Prepare it for the sky.ן¿½


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This artless vow may heaven receive,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And you, fond maid, approve:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So may your guiding angel give
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whateן¿½er you wish or love!


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So may the rosy-fingerן¿½d hours
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lead on the various year,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And every joy, which now is yours,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Extend a larger sphere!


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And suns to come, as round they wheel,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your golden moments bless
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With all a tender heart can feel,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or lively fancy guess!
meaning:
About: William Cowper was an English poet and hymnodist. One of the most popular poets of his time, Cowper changed the direction of 18th century nature poetry by writing of everyday life and scenes of the English countryside. 
He was a nephew of the poet Judith Madan.
